entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 02 - Content for common reuse types
Re: How to configure business goals that can change in time
entry.content: For each plan, the section weight needs to be 60% for business goals and then 40% for individual goals.   If you need to convert the goal weights from adding to 60% to adding to 100%, multiply the existing weight by 5 and then divide by 3.   For example, 30% would multiply by 5 to make it 150% and then divide by 3 to give you the proper result of 50%.

entry.title: Manual Value Cases: Track Transformation Value From Any Source
entry.content:
What Manual Value Cases Deliver

Flexibility in value capture: Transformation Leads can now create value cases directly within an Initiative. Define the metric, set baseline and target values, specify the time period—all within the familiar Transformation Management experience.

Consistent measurement: Manual Value Cases use the same metric types as PAM-sourced value cases:

  • Monetary values (cost savings, revenue impact)
  • Non-monetary values (efficiency gains, quality improvements)
  • Duration units (days, weeks, months, hours, minutes, seconds)

Unified visibility: All value cases—whether sourced from PAM or entered manually—appear together in the Value Cases table. Each value case clearly indicates its source, maintaining transparency for reporting.

Data integrity: Manual Value Cases are separated from PAM-sourced value cases at the data level. This preserves the integrity of automated value tracking while extending your ability to capture the full transformation story.

Designed for Transformation Leads

The workflow is straightforward:

  1. Navigate to any Initiative
  2. Open the Value Cases table
  3. Create a new manual value case
  4. Define your metric type, baseline, and target values
  5. Set the relevant time period

The side panel guides you through each step. Edit or delete value cases as your transformation evolves. 

 

 

For detailed guidance, visit the SAP Help Portal .

Coming soon:

  • Currency conversion — Handle monetary values across currencies, consistent with how PAM value cases work today
  • Faster value case creation — Streamlined workflows to connect value cases to your process landscape
  • Improved discoverability — Better guidance for teams getting started with value tracking

entry.title: Split Billing Documents Posting Into Multiple Accounting Documents
entry.content:
We understand that in SAP S/4HANA there is standard functionality available for billing document accounting split (GLO_IN_BILL_ISSUE_SPLIT / FI_BILL_ISSUE_SPLIT) which helps split FI accounting documents generated from SD billing documents when FI line item limits(999) are exceeded.

We have a similar business requirement in our ECC 617 system, where high-volume billing documents are causing FI document line item limitations.

entry.title: Re: Requirement for Project-wise Customer and Supplier Li...
entry.content:
Dear @sivakumar_k571 

Did you try already the following blog post ?
Custom fields for the "Manage Customer Line Items"... - SAP Community

This blog post describes an example how to use a custom field called "Responsible Unit" to manage customer line items. To fill the custom field, the extension point Journal Entry Substitution (FIN_ACDOC_SUBSTITUTION) is used.

You could map this example to the described requirement. Prerequisite to do so is, that you can define a custom logic  which can derive the project information based on the data of the Journal Entry. The extension point Journal Entry Substitution (FIN_ACDOC_SUBSTITUTION) provides as input the the Journal Entry header as well as all Journal Entry Line Items. That is you can for example also analyze the revenue line items to derive a value for the custom field in the customer line item.

Best regards
Steffen Riemann

entry.title: Re: Connection between SAC Analytics Cloud and SAP HANA C...
entry.content:
@Stephany429  Yes, this connection is possible. It mainly depends on whether you want to use a live connection or create an imported model in SAC.

For SAP HANA Cloud on BTP, SAC supports a native live connection. In this case, the data stays in SAP HANA Cloud and SAC consumes it directly. For this setup, you do not need the SAP Analytics Cloud Agent or the SAP Cloud Connector.

SAP documentation:
Live Data Connection to SAP HANA Cloud

If you are trying to create a standard SAC model by importing data from SAP HANA, then the setup is different. For an import connection to SAP HANA, SAP requires the SAP Cloud Connector, SAP Analytics Cloud Agent, and SAP Java Connector.

SAP documentation:
Import Data Connection to SAP HANA

However, if your model is created in SAP Datasphere, for example as an Analytic Model, then I would recommend connecting SAC directly to Datasphere using a live connection, instead of connecting SAC to the underlying SAP HANA Cloud database.

SAP documentation:
Live Data Connections to SAP Datasphere

So, for our scenario, if the data model is already available in Datasphere or HANA Cloud and we only want to consume it for reporting in SAC, the live connection would be the simpler and cleaner option. The SAC Agent and Cloud Connector would only be relevant if we specifically need an import-based connection.

entry.title: Joule Studio at SAP Sapphire 2026: Ten Days, Two Cities, One Clear Signal
entry.content:
As a Product Manager for SAP Build, focused on Early Adopter and Product Enablement, SAP Sapphire is one of the most important moments in the year - not just to present what we have built, but to hear directly from the people building with it. This year, the SAP Build PM team was deeply embedded across both events: in the ASUG pre-conference, the keynotes, the sessions, and the Agent Lab. We asked our PM colleagues to do what product managers do best - observe closely, listen carefully, and capture what they saw. This post is the result of that: a collective picture of what Joule Studio's debut at Sapphire looked and felt like, from the people who were there.

SAP Sapphire 2026 was held across two cities - Orlando (May 11–13) and Madrid (May 19–21) - and the energy in both was unmistakable. Joule Studio made its public debut at Sapphire. Thousands of customers, partners, and developers experienced it hands-on: some for the first time, some building on what they had already seen in Florida. The conversation that started in the US continued in Europe with the same momentum and the same conclusion. Enterprise AI has stopped being a promise and started being something you can build in an afternoon.

 

Orlando: Learning by Building

SAP Sapphire Orlando set the tone for what this year's event would be about. Christian Klein opened the keynote with a question that reframes everything: will SAP even be a software company in the future? His answer - that SAP is becoming a business AI company - was not defensive. It was clarifying.

The core argument landed clearly: AI keeps stalling in enterprises not because the models are lacking, but because they are trained on the public internet, not on your business. They have no knowledge of your processes, your data, or your compliance framework. SAP Business AI Platform - and Joule Studio within it - is built around fixing exactly that.

Christian Klein introducing SAP Business AI Platform during the Keynote

Joule Studio was introduced as an intent-based, deeply contextualized build environment for agents, open to any third-party model with no lock-in and both low-code and pro-code paths - with agent development free and backed by a €100M+ ecosystem investment. Throughout the event, there was a noticeable shift away from isolated AI use cases toward AI-infused end-to-end experiences. Attendees were not looking for another AI experiment - they wanted proof that this works in production. Real-world customer journeys, with Fujitsu cited as a concrete reference multiple times, drove that conversation.

Muhammad Alam introducing SAP Autonomous Suite during the Keynote

The keynote by Dr. Michael Ameling, Jonathan von Rüden, Jan Oberhauser, André Christ, and Maria Kondratyeva featured a live Joule Studio demonstration that became the defining moment of the event. The team demonstrated a 3-way invoice matching agent built end-to-end on stage. The scenario was immediately relatable: a company processing 50,000 invoices monthly, chronic matching exceptions, and approvals consistently landing with the wrong people.

Joule Studio received a natural language description of the problem. What followed was automated and complete: SAP LeanIX integration discovering the relevant SAP systems, SAP Signavio applying 50 years of SAP process expertise to understand matching strategies and routing rules, generated orchestration workflows for exception handling and escalation, and Agent Hub providing governance from the first deployment.

The result: Accounts Payable (AP) exception cycle time reduced from 72 hours to 90 seconds. Five manual touchpoints eliminated to zero.

One audience reaction said it all: “I couldn't tell if it was a real system or a guided demo.”  That comment - part skepticism, part disbelief - repeated throughout the week. The bar for proving production-readiness is high. Joule Studio cleared it.

Christian Klein introducing the Autonomous Enterprise during the Keynote

 

The Agent Lab: From Skepticism to Conviction

If the keynote introduced Joule Studio, the Agent Lab made it personal. Over three days, more than 1,000 unique customers and partners queued up and nobody left early. The numbers are striking: 3,366 AI solutions created, 1,640 agents built, across Finance, HR, Supply Chain, and beyond.

The Agent Lab in Orlando

The experience followed a consistent pattern: skepticism at the start, hands-on building in the middle, and genuine surprise at the end. The barrier was never ideas - it was trust that the platform would actually work. Once they saw their own business problem translated into a working design, that trust arrived instantly.

As Samir Hamichi from the Agent Lab team observed: “People were looking to know the added value from SAP, what [was] the differentiator. When they walked through the experience, they understood the power of tools and strategy.”

The Agent Lab in Orlando

 

What Makes Joule Studio Different

Every AI coding tool on the market can write code. What they cannot do is understand your business. Generic tools operate at the file and repository level - they have no knowledge of your procure-to-pay process, your enterprise architecture, or the SAP capabilities already available to you. Context is lost at every handoff.

Joule Studio changes the starting point entirely. The SAP Knowledge Graph, process models from SAP Signavio, enterprise architecture from SAP LeanIX, and SAP Domain Models all flow automatically into every development artifact from the first session. A business analyst describes a problem in plain language. Joule Studio generates a structured, SAP-context-aware Product Requirements Document - a shared source of truth for business stakeholders, developers, and architects, aligned from the first output rather than after weeks of back-and-forth. That PRD becomes a Technical Specification; the specification becomes production-grade, clean-core-aligned code, deployable to a fully managed runtime.

The insight heard repeatedly across the event from customers: “This brings business back into the loop.” And from one partner architect: “Context at scale - Signavio, LeanIX. That is powerful.”

One participant shared on social media what struck with him most: the end-to-end completeness. From describing the intent to generating a PRD, functional and technical specifications, unit tests, and a deployable solution - all without leaving the platform. What surprised him most was that the platform itself guides you through the process, asking clarifying questions like a colleague would rather than expecting you to have all the answers upfront. As he put it, you are not just building agents - you are building them with the help of agents.

You are not building an agent that connects to your business afterward. You are building one that is grounded in it from the first sentence you type.

SAP Business AI Platform

 

The Governance Conversation Nobody Skips

One exchange at the Orlando Agent Lab deserves to be named directly. An attendee, after seeing what agents could do, said: “Now I can cut half my company.”

Maria Kondratyeva pushed back immediately: “That is not what agents are for. Agents eliminate soul-crushing manual work - data entry, chasing approvals, reconciling exceptions - so people can focus on work that requires judgment, creativity, and relationship-building. The work only humans can do.”

But the fact that the question was asked reflects how seriously people are taking this. The governance features in Joule Studio - Agent Hub, LeanIX integration, and human-in-the-loop approval gates - are not compliance checkboxes. They are what separates enterprise AI from unmanageable risk. The governance story needs to be as prominent as the capability story - and across both cities, the sessions that included it landed better every time.

 

Madrid: The Momentum Crosses the Atlantic

One week later, Madrid picked up exactly where Orlando left off. Dr. Michael Ameling, Jonathan von Rüden, Jan Oberhauser, André Christ, and Soufia Maherzi brought the same invoice matching demonstration to a European audience and got the same reaction. A fresh crowd, the same disbelief turning into belief.

The Madrid Agent Lab wrapped up with 765 customers and partners, 1,350 AI solutions created, and 770 agents built. Different city, same energy: long queues, nobody leaving, and that consistent moment of surprise when an attendee's business problem became a fully designed agent before their eyes.

As Friederike Marby recalled: “Agent Lab was intense in the best possible way. The sheer volume of feedback, the questions, the ideas flying around. We had customers who flat-out didn't believe it was real, almost looking for reasons to dismiss it. But what really stayed with me was watching them discover, on their own terms, that it absolutely was.”

 The Madrid Agent Lab Team

 

Final Thoughts

What struck us most, putting this post together, was the consistency of what our colleagues reported across both cities - independent observations that told the same story. The skepticism at the keynote. The energy in the Agent Lab. The moment attendees stopped asking “can this really work?” and started asking “what do I build first?”

That shift - from doubt to conviction, not through a polished pitch but through direct, hands-on experience - is exactly what we set out to enable. Joule Studio's debut at Sapphire was not just a product launch. It was the beginning of a much larger conversation between SAP and the enterprise community about what it means to build with AI in a way that is grounded, governed, and genuinely useful.

We are grateful to our PM colleagues whose impressions and observations brought this post to life - and to every customer, partner, and developer who showed up, asked hard questions, and built something.

 

What is Next

Across two cities and six days of hands-on building, the combined numbers tell their own story: thousands of agents created, thousands of attendees who arrived skeptical and left with a deployment plan.

Joule Studio is planned to reach General Availability in Q3 2026. The trial is free, and the starting point is a business problem you already know you have.

 

Want to watch the sessions?

- Listen to the keynote - The Beginning of Better

- Discover the Demo - The Business AI Platform for your future

- Watch the Deep Dive - From intent to execution: Build AI agents with Joule Studio

- Hear the Strategy - The enterprise path to agentic AI

All Sapphire recordings are available here

Register for upcoming webinars here

 

Ready to start building?

Visit Joule Studio to learn more and register your interest.

Explore the Autonomous Enterprise Learning Journey for guided tutorials and deeper dives into specific capabilities.

The enterprise AI era is not approaching. In Orlando and Madrid, SAP customers and partners are already building it, one agent at a time.

entry.title: Re: Upgrade SAP Cloud Connector to 2.19.0
entry.content:
Hi,

we have the same problem and SAP answered on this:

At present, version 2.19.0 has not yet been released for use in ECS. The responsible ECS team is still validating the version as part of the internal release management process. Therefore, an upgrade to this version is currently not possible.

Once the version is approved and released by ECS, it will become available in the upgrade options accordingly.

entry.title: Re: Offsetting maintenance items in a strategy plan.
entry.content:
Hi, 

Your scheduling happens at the Maintenance plan level, not in the item maintenance item level. This is not possible with this setup. 
You need to create separate maintenance plans and schedule based on the different dates. 

Regards

Terence

entry.title: Secondary Cost G/Ls Not Visible in Project Costs App
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

We are using SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and have observed that in the Project Costs app, only values posted to Primary Cost and Revenue G/L accounts are displayed. Postings to Secondary Cost G/L accounts are not shown, although they are visible in the Display Line Items app.

Our understanding is that the Project Costs app considers only the G/L accounts assigned in YPS2, and since only Primary Cost and Revenue G/Ls are maintained there, Secondary Cost G/L postings are excluded.

Could you please confirm:

  1. Is this standard system behavior?
  2. Is it correct that YPS2 should contain only Primary Cost and Revenue G/L accounts?
  3. Should Secondary Cost G/Ls also be assigned in YPS2 if they need to be reported in the Project Costs app, or is that not the recommended approach?

Thank you for your guidance.

Rohini Dutta

Positioning SAP Business Data Cloud for the Retail industry
entry.content:
Retail leaders are being asked to do more with less. More personalization. More resilience. More visibility. Yet only 34% of business leaders fully trust their data.

That gap creates a huge opportunity for SAP Partners.

In our upcoming webinar, Positioning SAP Business Data Cloud for the Retail Industry, we'll go beyond product messaging and explore how SAP Business Data Cloud fits into a modern retail architecture.

We'll cover how SAP BDC brings together SAP and non-SAP data, supports predictive replenishment and demand forecasting, enables the data product economy, and lays the foundation for autonomous retail powered by Agentic AI.

Plus, you'll see a live demo and learn how to connect these capabilities to executive priorities across merchandising, pricing, inventory, and supply chain operations.

Whether you implement solutions, build extensions, or lead customer conversations, this session will help you position SAP BDC with greater confidence and credibility.

entry.title: how to custom agent for sales price approval
entry.content:
Hi sap community,

Regarding the sales price approval function, I am currently testing two scenarios:

1. Scenario 1: Custom Team Type with Custom Responsibility Definition I created a custom team type with one standard responsibility definition and one custom responsibility definition named "Price Channel" (which contains two sample values, A and B). I then set up two separate teams—one assigned to custom responsibility definition value A and the other to value B—in order to split the approval routing between the teams.

  • The Issue: However, during testing, the system still sends approval notifications to all users assigned as "Sales Price Approver – Level 1" across both teams, according to the settings in the Manage Sales Price Workflows app.

2. Scenario 2: Creator-based Approver Determination (If Creator is A, Approver is B) I am testing a case where if the document creator is User A, the designated approver must be User B. To achieve this, I have configured the responsibility rule, context, and custom responsibility rule logic (BAdI/Custom Logic).

  • The Issue: Similar to Scenario 1, the system completely ignores the filters and continues to blast approval notifications to all users belonging to "Sales Price Approver – Level 1" in both teams based on the workflow configuration.

Could you please help point out what might be causing this root issue, and how I can achieve the expected behavior for both scenarios?

Thank you in advance for your support!

entry.title: EVENT_PARAMETER cuts long strings at 255 characters
entry.content:
I have an event in SQL Anywhere 17 and trigger it with parameters.

Inside the event I use the function EVENT_PARAMETER to get access the passed parameters. This works fine as long the passed parameter has less than ~4000 characters, otherwise it reads only the first 255 characters and ignores the rest.

Has anyone this problem and maybe have a workaround/solution for it (other than -gp server option)?

entry.title: BTP IntegrationSuite SalesfrocePubSub adapter limits/costs
entry.content:
We are currently implementing integrations between Salesforce and SAP ECC using SAP Integration Suite / CPI and the Salesforce Pub/Sub adapter.

Our current design is event-driven, where Salesforce outbound interfaces publish events to Salesforce Pub/Sub queues/topics, which are then consumed by CPI and forwarded synchronously to SAP ECC through XI/proxy interfaces.

At the moment, the expected landscape would include approximately 20 different Salesforce event queues/topics, which would likely translate into around 20 Salesforce Pub/Sub adapter consumer instances/connections running in CPI.

We would like to better understand the potential operational and performance implications of this architecture on the SAP BTP Integration Suite tenant.

Specifically, we would appreciate guidance regarding:

  • Expected impact of multiple concurrent Pub/Sub adapter subscriptions on CPI runtime resources
    Memory/thread/worker consumption considerations for persistent gRPC streaming connections
  • Recommended sizing or scalability limits for Salesforce Pub/Sub consumers
  • Any known limitations, recommendations, or customer experiences regarding large-scale Salesforce Pub/Sub adapter usage

entry.title: Inquire about PO's with milestones (%) from PR's with totals (values)
entry.content:
Hello,

I would like to ask you about the following business scenario that we have and if you could tellme if there is a Standard solution for it.

From a Purchase Requisition with X positions in which each positions is a different Item with a value, we need to convert it into a PO with the same positions. But it is needed to reflect in the header the many number of milestones (percentage for payment that add up to 100%) that represent the invoices that will arrive from the supplier in different dates.

It is needed because the integration with VIM needs those percentages values to be able to automatically post the invoices when they arrive as they will not have the value of the items nor the GR, it will have the percentage value.

¿Is there a standard way of doing this that could integrate with MM-FI-VIM?

Example:

PR:

4 positions - 1 for 50€ / 1 for 300€ / 1 for 500€ / 1 for 150€ (Total 1000€)

PO:

4 positions - 1 for 50€ / 1 for 300€ / 1 for 500€ / 1 for 150€ (Total 1000€)

3 milestones - 35% 350€/ 40% 400€/

entry.title: Preventing Negative Stock Postings in WM Interim Storage Types
entry.content:
Hi Team,

We are facing stock inconsistencies in WM due to pending WM activities (TR/PCN to TO conversion and confirmation), which eventually result in negative stock situations in interim storage types.

To prevent this, I tried disabling the "Allow Negative Stocks" indicator in the storage type configuration. However, the system still allows negative stock postings in the interim storage type when goods issue is posted.

Could you please advise on the following:

1. Is there any standard SAP configuration to completely prevent negative stock postings in WM interim storage types?
2. Why does the system continue to post negative stock even after disabling the Allow Negative Stocks setting for the interim storage type?
3. Are there any additional WM controls, exits, BADIs, or validations available to block such postings?
4. What is the SAP-recommended approach to ensure IM and WM stock remain synchronized and avoid stock inconsistencies caused by pending WM activities?

Any relevant SAP Notes, best practices, or recommendations would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you,
Leelamohan

entry.title: PartnerEdge Build: SAP Transportation Management (TM) Not Appearing in Price List
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

We are an ISV registered under the SAP PartnerEdge Build program and are working to build a certified integration between our platform and SAP Transportation Management (SAP TM) embedded in S/4HANA.

Our product is a freight market intelligence and data platform — we deliver real-time spot rates, capacity indices, and carrier benchmarking data via API. The use case for SAP TM users is surfacing this market data natively within transportation planning and carrier selection workflows.

The problem: We cannot find the SAP TM partner test/dev environment anywhere in our PartnerEdge price list.

Here is the navigation path we have exhausted:
• Categories → Cloud - S/4HANA Applications → Line of Business Portfolio → Cloud - S/4HANA Digital Supply Chain
• The only three products listed there are SAP Project and Resource Management (8021070), SAP Service and Asset Manager (8005710), and SAP Intelligent Product Recommendation (8016066) — none of which are relevant to Transportation Management.

We have also checked:
• Cloud - S/4HANA Digital Supply Chain (top-level shortcut) — same three products
• Cloud - S/4HANA Digital Enterprise Platform
• Partner Test, Demo, and Development On-Premise

We have already contacted the SAP Partner Portal support team, who were unable to resolve this and redirected us here.

Our specific questions:
1. What is the correct product SKU / license for an SAP PartnerEdge Build partner to access an SAP TM development and test environment on S/4HANA Cloud?
2. Does SAP TM require a separate entitlement request outside the standard price list — i.e., does it need to be manually enabled by SAP's partner team?
3. Is SAP BTP (Business Technology Platform) a prerequisite that needs to be provisioned first before TM becomes visible?
4. Has anyone else run into this and found a resolution path?

Any guidance from partners who have successfully navigated this, or from SAP product/partner team members, would be greatly appreciated. Happy to share more detail about our integration use case if helpful.

Thank you.

entry.title: ALM Google Chat implementation - Method doesn't allow unregistered callers
entry.content:
We are configuring a Webhook notification from SAP Cloud ALM to a Google Chat Space using an HTTP BTP Destination (NoAuthentication).

We got the following error:

Configuration error occurred : {
  "error": {
    "code": 403,
    "message": "Method doesn't allow unregistered callers (callers without established identity). Please use API Key or other form of API consumer identity to call this API.",
    "status": "PERMISSION_DENIED"
  }
}

 

Has anyone encountered this exact issue when setting up Google Chat webhooks via BTP Destinations for SAP Cloud ALM? If so, were you able to resolve it, or did you find a working workaround? Any help or insights would be highly appreciated!

entry.title: Re: Split Billing Documents Posting Into Multiple Account...
entry.content:
Go to SPRO > Sales and Distribution > Billing > Billing Documents > Country/Region-Specific Features > Maintain Maximum Number Of Billing Items. Here, you have the options to restrict the number of line items in billing.

In ECC 617, there is no standard FI-side split functionality like in S/4HANA. The only way to meet the requirement is through custom development either by enhancing FI posting logic (via BAdIs/exits) or by redesigning aggregation rules to reduce line items.

entry.title: Re: Preventing Negative Stock Postings in WM Interim Stor...
entry.content:
Hello @Leelamohan 

I think you have made the correct configuration. But there is one more piece to the puzzle. Check the documentation of the "Allow negative stock" field:

You also need to customize L9040 message status - SPRO: Interfaces -> Inventory Management -> Allow Negative Stocks in Interim Storage Types -> Control of System Messages in Warehouse Management.

The standard setting is blank, meaning no message. Change it to E and retest.

Technical explanation

Procedure CHECK_NEGATIVE_STOCK_ALLOWED, include LLCMBF1D verifies if negative stock is allowed:

      CALL FUNCTION 'ME_CHECK_T160M'
           EXPORTING
                I_ARBGB = 'L9'
                I_MSGNR = '040'
                I_MSGVS = PARAM_MSV
           EXCEPTIONS
                ERROR   = 1
                NOTHING = 2
                WARNING = 3
                OTHERS  = 4.

      move sy-subrc to sav_subrc.                            "v_n_740871

      write: p_menge to lv_menge.
      concatenate lv_menge p_meins into lv_msgv2
                  separated by space.

      CASE sav_SUBRC.
        WHEN 1.
*         MESSAGE E040 WITH P_MATNR P_MENGE P_MEINS P_LGTYP.   "n_670743
          MESSAGE E040 WITH P_MATNR lv_msgv2 P_LGTYP P_LGPLA.
        WHEN 3.
*         MESSAGE W040 WITH P_MATNR P_MENGE P_MEINS P_LGTYP.   "n_670743
          MESSAGE W040 WITH P_MATNR lv_msgv2 P_LGTYP P_LGPLA.
                                                             "^_n_740871
      ENDCASE.

The message is ignored if its status is not E or W.

Best regards

Dominik Tylczynski

entry.title: I am unable to delete BP relationships 33099736 and 38085025 due to active transactions.
entry.content: The message indicates that the Business Partner (BP) relationships 33099736 and 38085025 have active transactions and cannot be deleted. This is standard SAP behavior: BP relationships involved in active transactions or documents cannot be deleted until those transactions are closed, completed, or archived. The issue occurs when trying to delete these relationships, likely in SAP for Me or related systems.

entry.title: E-Reporting Vs E-Invoicing for External Billing Documents
entry.content:
Hi,

We are trying to establish a link between E-Invoicing versus E-Reporting for Invoices which are coming to SAP DRC from non-SAP system. Non-SAP systems feeds the invoice data to SAP DRC in UBL 2.1 format and then SAP DRC creates its e-Document .

In the BP we maintain country key=FR and FR2 (SIREN) with taxstatus=PRA hence making the customer as established entity in France. so it qualifies for the Domestic B2B transaction and hence the E-Invoicing.

In some customers we maintain country key=FR and FR2 (SIREN) with taxstatus=NOR hence making the customer as Non-established entity in France. so it qualifies for the Domestic B2C transaction and hence E-Reporting.

For SAP native invoices i-e Billing document generated via SD or FI postings, above BP logic is working fine and it is routing the invoice data correctly to E-Invoicing or E-Reporting folder.

For the invoices coming from non-SAP system as UBL invoice, necessary business transformation is happening in the middleware and then it is replicated to SAP DRC and SAP DRC is creating e-Invoice for every case disregarding the FR2 TAXSTATUS=PRA OR NOR.  I am not sure why SAP standard logic does not work  for these invoices coming from external systems. Has anyone encountered this issue ? Will SAP standard logic not work for non-SAP invoices coming in UBL 2.1 format and we will have to do this transformation in middleware ?

Any experiences will be appreciated.

Thanks

Atif

entry.title: Re: What is clean core anyway?
entry.content:
@Denver_Fletcher91  - Great point...  A clean core (all five principles) is essential to run an effective autonomous enterprise. Business context and data are foundational for utilizing agents effectively.

  • Using standard processes (best practices) provides the ability to utilize out-of-the-box AI that already has a great business context.
  •  It emphasizes reliable data and semantics.  AI quality depends on consistent master/transactional data and end‑to‑end telemetry.
  • Governing extensibility and integration are important so the agents interact via published APIs/events on SAP BTP, avoiding brittle exits. 
  • Clean core provides a stable upgrade path so agents keep working when your core is updated, because they’re not tied to in‑core custom code. 
  • Using Agent Hub will also provide a governance framework for using agents in the context of business processes (And hopefully avoid sprawl of agents 😊)

entry.title: Why Business Readiness Is the Most Overlooked Success Factor in SAP S/4HANA Transformations
entry.content:
Why Business Readiness Is the Most Overlooked Success Factor in SAP S/4HANA Transformations

Introduction

When organizations discuss SAP S/4HANA transformation success, the conversation often focuses on data migration, testing completion, system performance, and cutover execution. While these areas are critical, one factor consistently determines whether a transformation delivers its intended business value: business readiness.

Over the years, I have observed SAP programs that successfully completed testing, migrated data accurately, and executed go-live activities on schedule, yet still struggled after deployment. In many of these situations, the technology was ready, but the business was not.

Users were uncertain about new processes, support teams were not fully prepared, decision-making responsibilities were unclear, and adoption challenges emerged immediately after go-live.

This experience has reinforced an important lesson: technical readiness and business readiness are not the same thing.

The Myth of Technical Readiness

Many SAP transformation programs use technical milestones to determine go-live readiness.

Examples include:

  • System Integration Testing completed

  • User Acceptance Testing completed

  • Data migration validated

  • Interfaces tested

  • Cutover plans approved

These are important achievements, but they do not guarantee business success.

A technically successful implementation can still face significant challenges if users are not prepared to operate the new processes effectively.

Organizations often discover after go-live that readiness cannot be measured solely through technical indicators.

The Five Dimensions of Business Readiness

In my experience, business readiness can be evaluated across five key dimensions.

The Five Dimensions of Business Readiness

1. User Readiness

Users must understand how to perform their day-to-day activities in the new SAP environment.

Questions to consider include:

  • Have users completed training?

  • Do they understand new process flows?

  • Have they participated in testing activities?

  • Are they comfortable using the new system?

Strong user readiness reduces adoption challenges after go-live.

2. Process Readiness

Many SAP transformations introduce redesigned business processes.

Organizations should verify:

  • Process documentation is complete

  • Standard operating procedures are updated

  • Process ownership is clearly defined

  • Cross-functional dependencies are understood

Without process readiness, confusion often emerges during business operations.

3. Organizational Readiness

Transformation affects more than systems.

It often changes:

  • Roles and responsibilities

  • Approval workflows

  • Reporting structures

  • Operational procedures

Organizations should assess whether affected teams understand how their responsibilities will change after deployment.

4. Leadership Readiness

Leadership support plays a critical role in transformation success.

Business leaders should:

  • Understand transformation objectives

  • Communicate expected outcomes

  • Support organizational change

  • Participate in readiness reviews

When leadership engagement is weak, adoption challenges frequently increase.

5. Support Readiness

Go-live support teams must be prepared before production deployment.

This includes:

  • Help desk preparation

  • Escalation procedures

  • Knowledge articles

  • Incident management processes

  • Hypercare support models

Support readiness helps organizations respond quickly when issues arise.

Common Warning Signs

Several indicators often suggest that business readiness is weaker than expected.

Illustrative example of common business readiness warning signs that may increase user adoption risk, operational disruption risk, and overall go-live risk during SAP S/4HANA transformations.

Examples include:

  • Low training participation

  • Delayed business sign-offs

  • Limited testing involvement

  • Unclear process ownership

  • Frequent user concerns

  • Incomplete support documentation

These signals should be treated as readiness risks rather than administrative issues.

The earlier they are identified, the easier they are to address.

Measuring Business Readiness

One challenge organizations face is that business readiness is often evaluated subjectively.

A more effective approach is to establish measurable readiness indicators.

Example readiness categories include:

Readiness Area Example Metrics

User ReadinessTraining completion, assessment scores
Process ReadinessProcess documentation completion
Organizational ReadinessRole transition completion
Leadership ReadinessBusiness stakeholder participation
Support ReadinessSupport team preparedness

A readiness scorecard provides leadership with greater visibility into areas requiring attention before go-live.

Illustrative business readiness scorecard showing how organizations can measure readiness across user adoption, process maturity, organizational preparedness, leadership engagement, and support readiness before SAP S/4HANA go-live.

How AI Can Improve Readiness Monitoring

As organizations adopt AI capabilities, readiness monitoring can become more proactive.

Illustrative example showing how AI can analyze training metrics, user feedback, testing participation, support ticket trends, and business sign-offs to generate readiness insights, identify risks, and support informed go-live decisions.

AI can analyze:

  • Training participation trends

  • Testing activity levels

  • Business sign-off patterns

  • Support ticket trends

  • User feedback surveys

By identifying patterns and anomalies, AI can help predict areas where adoption challenges may emerge after go-live.

Rather than waiting for issues to occur, organizations can take corrective actions before deployment.

Business Readiness and Transformation Success

Organizations frequently invest significant effort in technical preparation.

However, transformation success ultimately depends on whether people, processes, and support structures are prepared to operate effectively in the new environment.

Business readiness should be reviewed with the same level of rigor as testing completion, migration validation, and cutover planning.

When organizations balance technical readiness with business readiness, they significantly improve the likelihood of achieving sustainable transformation outcomes.

Final Thoughts

SAP S/4HANA transformations are ultimately business transformations, not just technology projects.

While technical readiness remains essential, business readiness often determines whether users embrace the solution, operational disruptions are minimized, and expected business benefits are realized.

From my experience, the most successful SAP transformations are those that prepare not only the system for go-live but also the people, processes, leadership teams, and support organizations that will rely on it every day.

Business readiness may not always receive the same attention as testing or migration activities, but it remains one of the most important predictors of long-term transformation success.

entry.title: SAP TM - Transportation Management Enhancements: Data Products, Clean Core APIs & Performance
entry.content:
A deep dive into the OSS Notes shipping with SAP S/4HANA OP2025 Feature Pack Stack 2 (FPS2), covering Location and Org Unit Data Products, Transportation Order Clean Core Foundations, Business Data Cloud integration, Read API extensions, and delivery stage API capabilities.

Contents

  1. Introduction & Release Context
  2. Location Data Products & CDS Foundations
  3. Transportation Org Units Data Products
  4. Transportation Order: Clean Core & BDC Integration
  5. Read API Extensions & Lazy Assignment
  6. ASR Read API Performance Improvements
  7. Stage Information API for Deliveries
  8. Deployment Scope & Availability Matrix
  9. Summary

🚀 Introduction & Release Context

SAP S/4HANA OP2025 Feature Pack Stack 2

SAP S/4HANA OP2025 FPS2 continues a strategic trajectory that has been building across recent release cycles: making Transportation Management (TM) data consumable, extensible, and clean-core compliant. The OSS Notes released with this Feature Pack Stack reflect deliberate, coordinated investment across three distinct pillars — Data Products for SAP Business Data Cloud, Clean Core CDS and RAP objects for Transportation Orders, and targeted API performance and functional enhancements.

This blog synthesizes eleven OSS Notes into a coherent picture of what has changed, why it matters, and how it impacts customers running SAP S/4HANA on Public Cloud, Private Cloud, and On-Premise landscapes. Whether your focus is on analytics-ready data products, extensibility within the clean-core boundary, or improving integration performance, FPS2 delivers meaningful progress on all fronts.

Key Themes in FPS2 TM Development:
Location & Org Unit Data Products • Annotation Enrichment • Transportation Order Clean Core RAP Foundation • BDC Data Product Enablement (TOR) Lazy Assignment Read API Extensions • ASR Performance • Stage Information API per Delivery

🌏Location Data Products & CDS Foundations

OSS Notes: 3696891 • 3747480 • 3727007

Location master data is a cornerstone of Transportation Management. It underpins route determination, freight costing, carrier selection, and shipment scheduling. FPS2 formalizes Location data as a first-class Data Product consumable by SAP Business Data Cloud and external analytics platforms, backed by a clean, layered CDS architecture.

Core CDS Layer: Interface, Projection, and Data Provider Views

Note 3696891 establishes the foundational CDS view stack for Location data. Following the standard SAP VDM (Virtual Data Model) layering approach, new core interface views, consumption projection views, and dedicated data provider views for analytics have been introduced. This structured layering ensures that Location data can be exposed cleanly without breaking the extensibility contract defined by the clean-core paradigm.

  • New Location core interface CDS views — exposing master data fields as stable, versioned APIs
  • Data Product views aligned to the SAP Business Data Cloud consumption model
  • Data Provider views enabling OData-based, event-driven, and replication-based consumption

 Annotations for Location Data Products

Note 3747480 targets completeness annotation. -typed fields within Location Data Product CDS views receive formal Analytics and UI   ensuring that indicator fields (e.g., active status, geo-coding flags, location type indicators) are correctly typed, sortable, and filterable when projected into analytics tools such as SAP Analytics Cloud. This closes a gap where fields previously rendered incorrectly in downstream reporting.

Service Development for Location and Org Units Data Products

Note 3727007 covers the OData V4 service layer sitting above the CDS foundation. Dedicated service definitions and binding annotations are created so that Location and Org Unit Data Products are fully addressable via standard OData services. This enables:

  • Direct consumption by SAP Build Apps, SAP Integration Suite flows, and third-party tools
  • Consistent metadata-driven service behavior (filtering, sorting, $expand support)
  • Data replication targets for SAP Business Data Cloud integration scenarios

OSS 3696891

Location Core CDS Views, Data Product & Data Provider Views

Introduces the structured CDS view stack for Location master data. Establishes stable interface, consumption, and provider views aligned to the SAP VDM layering standard.

OSS 3747480

Location Data Product – Annotations

 Analytics and   annotations to Location Data Product CDS views, ensuring indicator fields render and behave correctly in analytics and reporting contexts.

OSS 3727007

Service Development for Location and Org Units Data Products

Creates OData V4 service definitions and binding annotations for Location and Org Unit Data Products. Enables direct service consumption by integration and analytics tools and provides the replication endpoint required for SAP Business Data Cloud scenarios.

🏛TM Data Products: Transportation Org Units

OSS Note: 3742552

Note 3742552 extends the Data Product framework introduced for Location entities to cover Transportation Organizational Units. In TM, Org Units represent the organizational structure governing freight management — including transportation planning points, shipping points, and carrier roles. Making these entities available as formal Data Products enables customers to build org-unit-aware analytics, workforce analytics overlays, and cross-functional reporting that bridges HR and TM structures.

  • New CDS Data Product views for Transportation Org Unit entities
  • Aligned data model with Location Data Products for consistent analytics join behavior
  • OData V4 service exposure enabling downstream consumption by SAP Business Data Cloud and external BI tools
  • Support for delta-enabled data replication for near-real-time analytics scenarios

Together, notes 3727007, 3696891, 3747480, and 3742552 deliver a complete, analytics-ready Location and Org Unit Data Product portfolio within SAP S/4HANA TM — a foundational capability for customers migrating to SAP Business Data Cloud.

🔒Transportation Order: Clean Core & BDC Integration

OSS Notes: 3619960 • 3691780 • 3688875 • 3620011

The Transportation Order (TOR) is the central business document in SAP TM represents a legally binding freight agreement between a shipper and a carrier. The FPS2 wave delivers significant investment in making TOR data cleanly accessible through SAP's standard RAP (RESTful Application Programming) model and available as a Data Product for Business Data Cloud.

Clean Core VDM CDS Views and RAP Objects for Transportation Processing

Note 3619960 introduces clean-core compliant VDM CDS views and RAP business objects for Transportation Processing. This is a foundational step: it means that access to TOR data no longer requires ABAP BAPI calls or direct table access — instead, it is channeled through stable, versioned, release-contract CDS entities and RAP behavior implementations. Customers building extensions stay inside the clean-core boundary, enabling smooth upgrades with FPS and support pack releases.

  • New VDM interface CDS views for Transportation Order processing entities
  • RAP behavior definitions enabling create, update, and action operations in a cloud-ready pattern
  • Stable OData service exposure with documented release contract
  • Foundation for BAdI-based extensibility within the clean-core model

Clean Core TOR Foundation Enhancement for OP25 FPS2

Building directly on 3619960, note 3691780 expands the foundation with additional TOR entity coverage and deepens the RAP implementation. Enhancements include additional projection views for specialized transportation processing steps, supplementary action implementations, and enriched metadata annotations for Fiori UI rendering. This note marks the FPS2 milestone for the ongoing clean-core TOR journey started in earlier feature pack stacks.

BDC Data Product for TOR – FPS1 Baseline (3620011) and FPS2 Expansion (3688875)

Notes 3620011 (FPS1) and 3688875 (FPS2) represent the Business Data Cloud (BDC) Data Product enablement track for the Transportation Order. While 3620011 established the initial TOR Data Product structure and replication-ready CDS views in FPS1, note 3688875 expands this for FPS2, adding:

  • Additional TOR header and item attributes exposed in the Data Product model
  • Extended BDC replication object definitions covering status fields, date/time attributes, and organizational assignments
  • Alignment with updated BDC Data Product framework conventions introduced in OP2025
  • Improved delta-handling support for incremental data replication to SAP Business Data Cloud

OSS 3619960

Clean Core TOR Transportation Processing VDM CDS + RAP

Foundational clean-core VDM CDS views and RAP business objects for Transportation Order processing, replacing BAPI/table-based access patterns.

OSS 3691780

Clean Core TOR Foundation Enhancement – OP25 FPS2

Expands CDS entity coverage and deepens RAP behavior for TOR in FPS2, including additional projection views and enriched Fiori annotations.

OSS 3620011

BDC / Data Product TOR – S/4 2025 FPS1

Establishes the BDC Data Product baseline for TOR in FPS1, providing replication-ready CDS views and initial BDC replication object definitions.

OSS 3688875

BDC / Data Product TOR – OP25 FPS2

FPS2 expansion of the TOR BDC Data Product, with additional header/item attributes, improved delta replication, and alignment to updated OP2025 BDC conventions.

Why this matters for clean-core adopters: With notes 3619960 and 3691780, SAP formally places the Transportation Order within the clean-core extensibility framework. Side-by-side extensions via BAdIs or custom CDS extensions remain upgrade-stable — a critical requirement for customers on the SAP cloud ERP journey.

🔁Read API Extensions for Lazy Assignment Scenarios

OSS Note: 3679607

Note 3679607 addresses a specific and common integration pattern in Transportation Management: lazy assignment. In lazy assignment scenarios, certain TOR-to-freight-unit or freight-unit-to-shipment assignments are not resolved at document creation time but are determined dynamically based on runtime conditions (e.g., available capacities, network constraints, carrier confirmations). This deferred resolution creates a challenge for Read APIs that expect fully resolved document state.

What Extension Delivers

The Read API extension introduced in 3679607 enriches the standard TM Read APIs to correctly handle and expose entities in lazy-assigned states:

  • Extended response structures that indicate lazy assignment status on freight unit and TOR entities
  • New filter parameters enabling API consumers to query specifically for lazily or fully assigned documents
  • Consistent handling of partially-resolved assignment chains — avoiding incomplete data returns that previously caused integration errors
  • Backward-compatible extension: existing integrations continue to function; new fields are additive

This improvement is particularly relevant for customers using SAP Integration Suite, third-party TMS integrations, or custom applications that consume TM document state via OData APIs and need to differentiate between fully committed and pending assignment states.

OSS 3679607

Read APIs Extension for Lazy Assignment Scenarios

Extends standard TM Read APIs to correctly handle lazy-assigned document states. Introduces assignment status indicators, new filter parameters, and consistent partial-assignment handling — enabling reliable integration with systems that consume TM document state via OData.

⚡ASR Read API Performance Improvements

OSS Note: 3759451

Advanced Shipment and Receipt (ASR) scenarios involve high-volume data exchange between SAP TM and execution systems such as Extended Warehouse Management, third-party WMS, and carrier portals. Read API performance directly impacts the throughput and reliability of these real-time integration flows.

Optimization Scope

Note 3759451 delivers targeted query performance optimizations for the ASR Read API. The improvements focus on:

  • Database access optimization: Refined CDS view join strategies and buffer utilization to reduce redundant database roundtrips
  • Result set reduction: Improved early filtering at the CDS layer, pushing selection criteria closer to the data source
  • Large document handling: Better pagination and streaming behavior for ASR documents with high item counts
  • Parallelism improvements: Where applicable, asynchronous fetch patterns for associated entity sets

Customers operating high-volume TM landscapes — particularly those in retail, consumer goods, and industrial manufacturing — will see the most significant benefit from these improvements, particularly during peak-period shipment processing windows.

OSS 3759451

ASR Read API Performance Improvements

Delivers targeted database and CDS-layer optimizations for ASR Read API queries. Reduces roundtrips, improves large-document pagination, and applies early result-set filtering — resulting in measurable throughput improvements for high-volume TM integration scenarios.

📦API for Stage Information per Delivery

OSS Note: 3737225

Multi-leg and multi-modal shipment scenarios in TM organize execution into stages — discrete legs of a transportation chain, each with its own carrier, mode of transport, and scheduling. While stage data has been available at the shipment document level for some time, retrieving stage-level detail specifically scoped to a delivery (outbound or inbound) required navigation through multiple document relationships.

What the New API Provides

Note 3737225 introduces a dedicated API endpoint that returns stage-level information directly in the context of a delivery document. This simplifies integration patterns significantly:

  • Single-call retrieval of all stages associated with a given delivery key
  • Stage attributes returned include stage sequence, departure/arrival location, carrier, scheduled and actual departure/arrival times, and transport mode
  • Aligned with the existing Delivery OData service namespace for consistent API surface area
  • Supports $filter on stage status, enabling consumers to query only active or completed stages

This API is particularly valuable for customer-facing track-and-trace applications, logistics control tower integrations, and EWM inbound delivery scheduling scenarios where the downstream consumer only holds the delivery number and needs to understand the full transportation chain behind it.

OSS 3737225

API for Stage Information per Delivery

New API endpoint exposing transportation stage data scoped to a delivery document. Provides stage sequence, location, carrier, timing, and transport mode in a single call — enabling simplified track-and-trace and logistics control tower integrations.

🏮Deployment Scope & Availability Matrix

Public Cloud • Private Cloud • On-Premises S/4HANA

The notes covered in this blog span all three SAP S/4HANA deployment models. The table below summarizes the availability of each capability area across deployment options based on the OSS Note scope declarations.

Summary

What FPS2 Delivers for TM Customers

SAP S/4HANA OP2025 FPS2 represents a well-coordinated release for Transportation Management, advancing three interconnected strategic priorities simultaneously.

For analytics and data platform customers, the Location and Org Unit Data Product investments (3696891, 3747480, 3727007, 3742552) deliver a clean, replication-ready data foundation that makes TM master data a first-class citizen in SAP Business Data Cloud. The formal annotation completeness in 3747480 may appear minor in isolation but directly impacts the quality of BI artifacts built on top of these data products.

For clean-core and integration architecture teams, the TOR Clean Core RAP foundation (3619960, 3691780) and BDC Data Product tracks (3620011, 3688875) mark a significant maturation. The Transportation Order — historically one of the most complex and heavily customized objects in TM — is being systematically brought into the clean-core extensibility model, reducing technical debt and upgrading risk for customers on the cloud journey.

For integration and operations teams, the Read API lazy assignment extension (3679607), ASR performance improvements (3759451), and stage information API (3737225) are practical, high-value improvements. They reduce integration workarounds, improve throughput in production TM environments, and enable simpler, more reliable track-and-trace and control-tower integrations.

Collectively, these eleven notes represent SAP's continued commitment to making SAP S/4HANA Transportation Management a modern, API-first, analytics-ready application — equally suited to public cloud SaaS consumption and on-premises enterprise deployments.

Hope that can help you.

Thanks, and regards

Rogerio Lima.

entry.title: Re: quantity Colum issue shown only positive value
entry.content:
Good day @Chinmay5 

Thank you for your question.

The new Material Documents Overview app is not a full replacement of every old MB51 analytical behaviour. It is mainly used to display material document items and document details.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/7cc07e548af58e4ce10000000a4450e5.html?locale=en-US

For totals, grouped quantities and stock movement analysis, Analyse Stock in Date Range is recommended because Material Documents Overview is not an analytical app and does not support the same total or grouping behaviour. See KBA 3649511 for more insights.

If the new Material Documents Overview app does not show the quantity sign in the same way as old MB51, there is no configuration switch confirmed to make it behave exactly like MB51. Use movement type and stock change fields for interpretation and use Analyze Stock in Date Range for stock movement analysis. KBA 3568133 confirms that you can switch back to MB51 from Material Document Overview where available. Also note that SAP has been closing gaps between MB51 and the new app.

I recommend you consider the following:

  • Compare the same material, plant, posting date and movement types in old MB51 and Material Documents Overview. 
  • Add Goods Movement Type, Stock Change and Stock Change List in the new app. 
  • Use Analyse Stock in Date Range for stock movement totals and stock analysis.    
  • Do not expect a configuration setting to change the Quantity column sign in Materials Documents Overview.
  • You may consider submitting an improvement request if the exact old MB51 signed quantity behaviour is mandatory in the new app

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: From XSUAA to AMS: Architecting S4HANA CAP Extensions with Policy-Based Authorization
entry.content:
Hello @sapstephan  

What I covered in the blog was mainly the application-side perspective (CAP + AMS integration and policy-based authorization model).

For user lifecycle management and mass assignment (UAR, bulk user-policy assignments, reporting, auditing, etc.), this is actually handled in the SAP Cloud Identity Services (Identity Provisioning / Identity Authentication administration layer) rather than inside CAP or AMS itself.

AMS focuses on:
- defining and evaluating authorization policies at runtime
- injecting effective roles/attributes into applications

But the operational governance layer (mass maintenance, provisioning, review workflows, audit reporting) is part of Cloud Identity Services tooling and admin processes, not the CAP/AMS programming model.

I would surely let you know if I explore more on this side of CIS.

entry.title: Re: Smartfield not rendered as ObjectStatus
entry.content:
It is the expected behavior. A field rendered in a smart form will be rendered as text in display mode and input in editable mode.

If you explicitly want Object Status, you might have to override the default rendering using Control Configuration.

<smartForm:controlConfiguration>

 

        <smartForm:ControlConfiguration key="State">

 

            <smartForm:customControl>
                <ObjectStatus
                    text="{StatusText}"
                    state="{
                        path: 'Status',
                        formatter: '.formatStatusState'
                    }"
                    icon="{
                        path: 'Status',
                        formatter: '.formatStatusIcon'
                    }"
                />
            </smartForm:customControl>

 

        </smartForm:ControlConfiguration>

 

    </smartForm:controlConfiguration>

entry.title: Re: Kerberos / SPNEGO Authentication with SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS)
entry.content:
Hi @dyaryura,

Thank you for the question.

I've  tried to test this scenario in my internal system.

Based on my tests, IAS does not seem to use a configurable authentication order. (except when using a conditional authentication)

  • If Entra ID is configured as the default IdP, IAS redirects the user directly to Azure and SPNEGO is not evaluated.
  • If IAS is the default IdP, IAS evaluates SPNEGO. If the user's IP matches the configured SPNEGO IP range and a valid Kerberos ticket is available, the user is logged in automatically. (using IP Ranges on SPNEGO configuration)
  • If IAS is default IdP (with conditional authentication to other IdPs) and SPNEGO is not applicable (for example, the user's IP is not in the allowed range), IAS displays its own login page instead of automatically redirecting to Azure.
  • Only after the user reaches the IAS login page can Conditional Authentication be evaluated and potentially redirect the user to Azure.

Therefore, SPNEGO does not appear to act as a first authentication method with automatic fallback to Azure. Instead, IdP routing and SPNEGO seem to be evaluated separately, with the default IdP taking precedence.

BR,
Arilsson

entry.title: Middleware Isn't Dead. It Grew Up.
entry.content:
The recent online conversation sparked by SAP's strategic investment in n8n has been energetic, and largely for the right reasons. The excitement is warranted. The investment is significant. But some of what's circulating online conflates a few things that are worth separating clearly — and some of the most important parts of the story are getting lost in the noise.

This post is my attempt to clear that up.

 

What the n8n partnership actually is

At SAP Sapphire 2026, SAP announced a strategic investment in n8n — the open-source workflow automation platform — as part of a multi-year commercial partnership with deep product alignment.

This is a genuine strategic move, and the reason is worth stating plainly: SAP didn't acquire a tool. SAP acquired a community. n8n brings 1.7 million active builders, 1,000+ app and system integrations, and a thriving ecosystem of workflows and templates built by people who were never traditional SAP developers. That's a different builder profile — technical generalists, citizen developers, automation engineers — and it's exactly the kind of expansion the SAP developer ecosystem has long needed. You no longer need to know ABAP or CDS views to build useful business automations.

For practitioners, what this means in practice is a visual, node-based workflow canvas embedded alongside the agent and app-building capabilities in Joule Studio, SAP's unified agent-building, app development, and automation environment within the SAP Business AI Platform. Builders who live in automation-first tools will feel immediately at home. The possibilities for connecting SAP agents to external services and building agentic workflows visually are genuinely compelling.

As we look to the future, expect SAP to continue to deepen this partnership. n8n's reach across the SAP landscape will grow, but more on that direction as it develops.

What the n8n partnership is not

Here is where some of the online commentary has gone sideways.

n8n in Joule Studio is a workflow automation and agent orchestration layer. SAP Integration Suite is an enterprise integration backbone. They solve fundamentally different problems, and treating one as a substitute for the other creates real architectural risk.

Integration Suite handles the things that have to work every time, at scale, under governance: enterprise-grade process integration, B2B EDI and trading partner management, full API lifecycle from design through monetization, hybrid and sovereign deployment through Edge Integration Cell, enterprise-grade event streaming, end-to-end error handling, SLA management, and audit trails for business-critical process flows. These are the scenarios where a visual workflow canvas is a complement, not a substitute.

The more useful frame is this: Joule Studio is where you build the agent, the app, or the workflow. Integration Suite is what lets those components reach every system, every process, and every partner across your enterprise — securely and at scale. The Joule Studio positioning is explicit about this: Integration Suite is named as one of the foundational layers that makes agents enterprise-ready. The two together are additive, and practitioners are better off for it.

The differing viewpoints in the online conversation are healthy

A range of perspectives has surfaced in these discussions — some overstating what n8n changes, some appropriately cautious about what it doesn't yet do. That kind of discourse sharpens everyone's thinking. But a few misconceptions keep appearing, and the most important one to address is the idea that n8n's arrival in Joule Studio resolves the PI/PO question.

It doesn't. Not yet, and not for most scenarios.

The PI/PO clock is real

SAP Process Integration and Process Orchestration reach end of standard maintenance in 2027, with extended maintenance available through 2030. That date is not moving.

The vast majority of PI/PO workloads are traditional integration scenarios — system-to-system message routing, B2B document exchange, ABAP proxy patterns, IDoc flows, RFC and JDBC lookups — that map directly and logically to SAP Integration Suite. These are not workflow automation scenarios. Migration tooling for these patterns exists today: the Migration Assessment tool is available on the free tier of Integration Suite and can give you a complete inventory and effort estimate of your PI/PO landscape without any commitment. The Migration Tool in Cloud Integration handles the conversion for the most common adapter patterns.

If you haven't started making a plan to migrate off PI/PO, it's not too late — but it's getting there. A large landscape with 300+ interfaces can take a year or more even with the best tooling. The time to start assessing is now, not when the maintenance window starts closing.

The good news: that migration is about to get meaningfully faster. SAP announced the PI/PO Migration Agent at Sapphire 2026, with general availability targeted for Q3 2026. This agent brings AI-driven automation to the medium and high complexity migrations that previously required the most specialist effort — the ones involving custom code, complex mappings, and logic that the existing Migration Tool couldn't handle automatically. For landscapes where migration friction has been the primary blocker, this agent compresses the execution phase significantly.

If you haven't started your assessment yet, there's no reason to wait. SAP's Migration Factory offers a free migration assessment to help you understand the scope, complexity, and effort of your PI/PO landscape before you commit to anything. Apply for your free assessment here.

The bigger picture

The n8n investment, the PI/PO Migration Agent, the MCP Gateway reaching general availability this quarter, the Joule conversational interface coming to Integration Suite in Q3 — these aren't separate announcements. They're part of a coherent direction: an integration platform that is becoming intelligent from the inside, connected to the entire enterprise, and accessible to a much broader builder community than ever before.

More tools. More flexibility. More paths to get your integration and automation landscape where it needs to be.

The middleware argument hasn't been killed. It's been answered — with options.

entry.title: Re: How to Implement SAP Business One projects successfully
entry.content:
Great breakdown on the implementation steps. Having run 20+ SMB go-lives in the Hyderabad market, I’d add one critical nuance: the go-live week is where SMBs lose the most money, and it’s almost never a technical issue. It’s a data readiness and visibility problem.

Three failure patterns I see repeatedly:

1. Master data validation is skipped under time pressure.

Teams migrate from Tally or legacy spreadsheets and the item master is “good enough” on cutover night. Three weeks later, inventory reports are useless because unit of measure conversions weren’t standardized. The fix: run a parallel inventory count in the old system AND SAP B1 for 2 weeks before go-live. Any variance >2% stops go-live until resolved.

The deeper issue here is that most teams don’t have real-time visibility into the data gaps. They’re comparing spreadsheets manually. If your team can’t see batch cost discrepancies or inventory variances in real time during the parallel run, you’re flying blind into go-live.

2. User training is treated as a checkbox.

SMB owners assume the consultant will “handle it.” What actually happens: 3 PowerPoint slides, a rushed demo, and a team that can log in but can’t process a goods receipt. The result: workarounds that corrupt data, followed by 6 months of cleanup.

Better model: identify 2–3 power users per department in week 1. Train them deeply. They become your go-live support army. Give them access to live dashboards — cashflow forecasts, inventory KPIs, supplier quality alerts — so they can validate transactions in real time instead of printing reports and cross-checking on paper.

3. No cutover rollback plan.

If something breaks on go-live night, what’s the decision tree? Who decides to rollback vs. push through? This sounds obvious but almost no SMB implementation documents this. The result: 48 hours of chaos before the inevitable rollback — which costs more than if you’d planned for it.

The Go-Live Checklist That Actually Works:

  • Parallel run confirmation — both systems running simultaneously for at least 5 business days, with real-time variance tracking (not end-of-day spreadsheet comparisons)
  • Data freeze window — no master data changes 48 hours before cutover
  • Batch cost gap analysis — run a full reconciliation of component costs, batch valuations, and inventory positions across both systems. If your batch costs don’t match within 1%, stop and fix before go-live
  • Rollback decision criteria — documented: who decides, under what conditions, with what data
  • Go-live support team — internal champion + consultant available for 72 hours straight, with access to live operational dashboards
  • First-week close check — finance runs month-end in SAP B1 within 5 days of go-live

The single best investment: A formal 3-day parallel run where the team processes every real transaction in BOTH systems simultaneously — with AI-powered batch cost gap analysis running in the background to flag discrepancies before they become post-go-live fires. Teams that skip this checklist average 3–4 weeks of disruption. Teams that use it average 3–4 days.

For pharma and manufacturing companies (especially those targeting USFDA or WHO-GMP compliance), add batch traceability validation to this checklist. Every goods movement during production should link a component batch to a production order to an FG batch. The genealogy chain has to be complete before your first regulatory audit, not during it.

What specific module are you struggling with? Happy to go deeper on any of these.

— Venkata Siva Reddy Polu, TEKROI
SAP B1 implementation for Hyderabad pharma and manufacturing SMBs

entry.title: Re: Receiving point not available for selection on sales...
entry.content:
Hello,

To have the receiving points available for selection in your sales document you need to set the receiving points in your customer master data under tab 'Customer: Unloading Points', scrolling down you can see a table for maintaining the receiving points.

Best regards.
